sections.title: General Guidance
sections.content: A fracture is a broken bone. Proper immobilization prevents further injury, reduces pain, and protects blood vessels and nerves until medical help arrives.

sections.title: Call Emergency Services Immediately If
items: Suspected head, neck, or spine injury
Limb looks pale, blue, or cold

sections.title: Signs of a Fracture
items: Severe pain at the injury site
Inability to move or bear weight
Bone protruding through skin (open fracture — emergency)

steps.title: Assess Injury
steps.subtitle: Check pulse, motor function, and sensation distal to injury. Do NOT move the injured area, keep the person still. Support the injured limb in the position found. Do NOT attempt to straighten the bone.

steps.title: Immobilize the Fracture
steps.subtitle: Immobilize the joint above and below the fracture. Arm Fracture - Use a splint and secure arm in a sling. Leg Fracture - Splint both sides of the leg. If no splint available, tie injured leg to uninjured leg. Suspected Spine Injury - Do NOT move the person unless in immediate danger. Stabilize head and neck. Wait for trained medical responders.

steps.title: Check for Bleeding
steps.subtitle: Control Bleeding (If Present), Apply gentle pressure with sterile gauze or clean cloth. For open fractures, do not push the bone back in, apply dressing around the wound carefully.

steps.title: Apply Splint
steps.subtitle: How to Make a Splint: Use rigid objects (wood, sticks, rolled newspaper, cardboard). Pad the splint with cloth, secure with bandages, cloth strips, or tape. Tie snugly but not too tight (check circulation).

steps.title: Reassess Circulation
steps.subtitle: Check circulation before and after Splinting. Check pulse below injury, and look for color and warmth. Ask about numbness or tingling.

steps.title: Pain Management
steps.subtitle: Provide over-the-counter pain relievers (if appropriate). Use wrapped ice pack for 15–20 minutes. Do NOT place ice directly on skin.
